displayTitle: 2025-12-04 - Environment Committee - 2024/25 Annual review of the Energy Efficiency and Conservation Authority [Part 3]
topic: 2024/25 Annual review of the Energy Efficiency and Conservation Authority
description: Thursday, 4th December 2025 | Environment Committee 2024/25 Annual review of the Energy Efficiency and Conservation Authority Hearing of evidence: Energy Efficiency and Conservation Authority Marcos Pelenur, Chief Executive Elena Trout, Board Chair Murray Bell, Group Manager Policy and Regulations William Jensen, Manager Policy and Engagement 2024/25 Annual review of the Environmental Protection Authority Hearing of evidence: Environmental Protection Authority Dr Allan Freeth, Chief Executive Barry O'Neil, Board Chair Sarah Watson, General Manager, Corporate Services Dr Lauren Fleury, Acting General Manager, Hazardous Substances and New Organisms Julia White, General Counsel Ross Perrin, Head of Finance and Business Services Richard Preece, Manager, Climate, Land and Oceans
descriptionSummary: 2024/25 Annual review of the Energy Efficiency and Conservation Authority. Energy Efficiency and Conservation Authority. Marcos Pelenur, Chief Executive

chunks.text: Well, welcome to the Environment Select Committee this afternoon, uh, for the annual review of the Energy Efficiency and Conservation Authority. Uh, we welcome you here. Uh, you have one hour with us, uh, this afternoon and, uh, members will have plenty of questions. But we'll hand it over to you to start with your opening remarks and insights. So thank you.

Mm-hmm. Um, ƒ10 koto ka tua, and thank you for the opportunity to appear before the committee today. I have the privilege of being the chair of, uh, EECA, and to my left is Dr Marcos Pelinao, who's the chief executor. As you're aware, EECA is the government's authority on energy efficiency and its delivery arm on the related programs. It's been globally proven that energy efficiency is instrumental in achieving more with less (1) and system, and provides better outcomes. I'm pleased to share with you today EECA's achievements for '24/'25, a year in which

EECA sharpened its focus to align with the government's priorities of strengthening energy security and improving energy affordability. The change in government's direction last year set a very clear expectation for EECA to concentrate on practical, market-based measures that reduce pressure on the energy system and to help keep costs down. In response, EECA streamlined its programs and prioritized the highest value interventions and accelerated work that directly supports greater security of supply and lower energy bills for New

Zealanders. EECA's key role is on the demand side of the energy system. Reducing unnecessary energy use is one of the most cost-effective ways to improve reliability and affordability. Every unit of energy eases pressure on the system and helps avoid higher costs to consumers. EECA's regulatory role is central to this. In '24/'25, EECA's energy efficiency standards and regulations delivered more than 1.5 petajoules of savings, which is equivalent to the annual use of power in over 36,000 homes, and it's

around $37 million in national benefits. The recently approved modernization of EECA's legislative framework will enable these two tools to expand even further to support system resilience and consumer affordability. Alongside the regulations, EECA continues to provide practical advice to inform households and businesses on how to reduce their energy costs and manage demand more effectively, particularly during peak periods when the network is under the greatest strength. These well-targeted information programs have actually demonstrated impact.

For instance, the Switch On Efficiency campaign delivered in the order of $2.50 return for every dollar invested, and the more recent Winter Energy Campaign actually had a bit higher return than that. EECA continues to work with industry to help bring forward new technologies, demand flexibility solutions, and new, new fuels into, such as woody biomass and biogas, because they offer real opportunities to reduce operating cost, defer supply-side investment, and strengthen system resilience. Looking ahead, EECA has realigned its '25/'26 program to focus squarely

on where it can deliver the greatest impact, improving energy affordability, and supporting a reliable and resis-resilient energy system, and contribute to New Zealand's economic wellbeing. We welcome any questions you may have of us. Brilliant. Thank you for that overview.

Uh, we'll go first to Megan Woods. Thank you. And I just ha-have a O primary and some supps around Warmer Kiwi Homes. Um, and thank you. I think one of the really useful metrics that EECA always does is calculating the value, um, that is generated out of that program and $4.36 in terms of wellbeing and energy, um, benefits. So, um, well worth money invested. Um, when does your funding for Warmer Kiwi Homes, what is your current funding for that program?

When is that through till? Mm-hmm. Go, it goes to the end of '26/'27. So there's no further funding in the year '27/'28. And the grants, uh, are about 55 million this year, 53 next year, and then 49 the year after that. Oh sorry. Sorry, I was looking at the wrong appropriation.

76 million and then 83 for the last two. Thank you. Um, are you doing work, um, around preparing cases for continued funding for Warmer Kiwi Homes? We think the case for Warmer Kiwi Homes, as you mentioned, uh, is really strong, but that's a decision for the government to make.

Right. Okay. Um, just in terms of, um, the targets within there, um, that there were some changes, um, to the criteria this year. Um, the... So in terms of the seven, eight, so the deciles seven, eight, and nine, which had been the original targets, how, how many of those target decile households had been reached do you think you got to around, um-... 80, 70, 80% of those houses?

For eight, eight and nine- Yeah. (clears throat) I th- Without giving a, a definitive answer, it is probably somewhere between 70 and 80% of those homes. And so since August 2025, we added deprivation areas five and six. Sure. Um, so we were really pleased to be able to expand the criteria and expand the program. It's just had a much s- dialed down grant portion, because those areas are more likely to be able to pay for, afford it.

And are they? What's the uptake like? It's good. There's still many homes left to do. There's h- hundreds of thousands of homes left to do. But in terms of the targets for this year? We are, uh, on track for the targets of this year. Mm-hmm. Good. One of the things, sorry, just one more, is that, um, there was previously funding for the hard-to-reach households, the Hard to Reach House- Households Program, because we know many of our most energy-deprived homes, there's multiple, there's multiple situations and layers happening within their household.

And actually having trusted providers going in and establishing those relationships, in terms of being able to continue in that eight and nine, um, decile, do you think we would need another dedicated fund to be able to make further inroads into the remaining 20 or 25% of those houses?

Those, I mean, they're hard to reach homes because they're hard to reach, so there's a general mistrust. Mm-hmm. Um, and I think if EECA just rocked up- Mm-hmm.... we might not have the door open. But we partner with- (clears throat)... community providers to help get that access. And- (clears throat)... the funds that we've taken from MBIE, the Community Resilience Energy Fund and the supporting, um, Energy Education and Communities Fund, we've used those to get the most, uh, access or improve our access with our program.

So, we've been going as far as we can to reach those homes with the funding that we have. Just got a supplementary on that from Cameron Luxon. Can you say why there's mistrust, did you say? Uh, I think it's- I mean, seriously. I think it's like- You seem like friendly people. We are friendly people. Um, I, n- not being in communities around New Zealand, just being Wellington-based means that it's hard, uh, for some, for an unknown person to come and just knock on somebody's door and open it.

So, what we found is if you can partner with a trusted messenger, like a community, like a community group or, uh, even just, um- So, it's, as you just said, there's general let, you know, distrust of people from Wellington. It's just- No, the government- Oh, the government. Oh, fine. The government are here to help, yeah. Yeah, oh, okay. And that's why the Warmakibi Homes- Mm-hmm.

... uh, Program has been so successful, because we do partner with community- Mm-hmm.... organizations which are actually touching these, these homes for various reasons. And it's been very successful over the life of the Warmakibi Home Program. And it's been in different guises, as the minister has raised. Um, you know, there's always changing of settings.

But- But that's one of the reasons why it has been very, very successful in New Zealand. There's also, um, we've also got feedback that, you know, because we're offering sometimes when we partner with third-party providers, like a free insulation for these homes, they don't trust the free. Mm-hmm. They're like, "There must be-" So, don't trust anything the government gives you for free. Yeah. "There must be, there must be something to it."

So, sometimes we'll partner with religious leaders or community leaders to just even say, "Hey, this offer is actually legit." That makes sense. Yeah. Yeah. There's nothing more expensive than something un... Yeah. And Megan, you've got a supplementary. I, I, just on that, I mean, I've had multiple phone calls as a constituent MP. People saying, "This crowd's just-"

You answered my question. No, I'm just saying that they've rung and said, "This, this crowd's offering me something free. Is it legit?" Yeah. So, they thought it- I thought you said it's going to cost you in the long run, They think it's, no, they think it's a scam. Fair enough then 'cause it is. Um- Well, it's not a scam. Next question, next question- No, no, no, I don't have-... is Scott Willis. Yeah, go Thank you, Madam Chair. Um- (laughs)... and I, and I appreciate your, your work with those third-party providers.

And we know there's a lot of mental health issues out there- Mm-hmm.... that make things challenging. Whanau navigators can be incredibly helpful. But my question is about the energy policy capability, because it received increased funding this year and is looking to get some more funding next year. So, given this increase, what progress has been made in establishing an energy wellbeing network?

Where are we, where are we at, and where's EECA, what's EECA's thinking on the establishment of an energy wellbeing network? And just to clarify so I've got it clear, when you talk about energy policy increases, that's the MBIE's energy policy appropriation? Yeah. Okay. Yeah. Because we, yeah, we don't have a energy policy program. Um, I think we need to work with community groups to del- to help us deliver our messages. So, we know that the work we did on- I just- ... investigating a community energy wellbeing network is that

there are a lot of potential duplication with groups using, you know, their own resources or having to generate their own resources. So, one of the things that we can do as an authority is provide really clear material and advice that essentially any group can pick up and use.

So, for, with that kind of support is how we would support a community energy wellbeing network. We don't have funding to fund one. Supplementary. Um, so, at the Energy Affordability and Resilience Conference last, earlier this year, there was, there was, um, a good discussion about the establishment of an energy wellbeing network.

How is EECA involved in enabling and catalyzing or in enabling-... that network to be established, given the strong call from the community energy sector. The need, the need that we see for that, that network and for that work to happen. Yeah. Look, we're really proud of, of the work we do with community groups and helping energy users. Empowering energy users sits right at the heart of the strategy. And so when I came in, when I looked at the organizational strategy, I think MBIE might do the strategic policy. You get your market regulators with the EA.

From the government lens, we are here to provide information so that energy users make the best choices for themselves, and businesses as well. And so a lot of that includes community groups. Uh, we've been able to do more with less when it comes to SEEK, so Supporting Energy Education in Communities.

We've been able to do more with less when it comes with Climate Resilience Energy Fund. And what I mean by that is rather than just having these solar and battery sit to be only used under emergencies, we've worked with community groups so that they can provide resilience all year round. Um, but saying that, the f- we don't have funding to s- stand up a energy wellbeing network. So our support would be using the authoritative advice that EECA has and being able to try and reduce the costs for those group- groups that are trying to

do good work in their communities. So being able to have access to shared resources, being able to share lessons with each other. Um, hopefully just kind of res- reducing or removing the transaction costs of them getting their work done. So we have a very small amount of funds that have been transferred from MBIE on the, what's called the Supporting Energy Education and Communities. It's about $2 million a year, and that will roll out the next couple of years. That's all the funds we have.

Mm-hmm. And I- I will say that it's worked well because we are, we are, all of our muscle is on delivery, you know? So we, we've got a whole bunch of technical advice and insights and analytic, uh, strength, but because we've got all of our groups and units set up for delivery, when we got those funds, we were able to just find a whole bunch of savings but still keep delivering really strong impacts. So the split between policy and delivery I think works very well between EECA and MBIE.

So a couple- Yeah, I just-... a couple of supplementaries.... had a supplementary. Yep. Yeah. So, um, and I get that, and I understand the work that EECA does. And I also understand that sometimes delivery isn't simply material intervention. It is also that, that education, that support, that that's where that community part plays a really big, big, big role, and it, and also in building the trust as you talked about earlier. So that, that thought of how do you apply resource to supporting building trust in the community to, to have the knowledgeable people who are

working at the flat roots to deliver for particularly those hard to reach? What, where is, where is the, the component of EECA's work that supports that bit? Um, go on. The Supporting Energy in Education and Communities Fund is probably, like, the main vehicle that we would use.

It's reached over 15,000 households. It's delivered 197 low-cost energy-saving devices. The independent evaluation that was done on it showed a $2.21 benefit per dollar spent, so we know the benefits are real. We think there's probably about 300,000 households in energy hardship, so there's a lot of, uh, people that can benefit from that kind of advice.

And SEEK is our main program for delivery. And, um, and on the, uh, data question that's related to what you- you mentioned earlier, uh, you have access to a number of, um, to a number of data points in relation to the Warmer Kiwi Homes program.

And I'm aware that EECA was heavily involved in the Energy Wellbeing Evaluation Consortium some time ago, and you've, you've provided in-kind support for the secretariat of that in the past. Is this something that EECA would look for, look towards to supporting for that, that data capture for building a greater knowledge base of, of the extent of energy hardship or wellbeing in the community? And, and if so, how would you look to continue to be involved?

Yeah. Well, without being able to see the details of the proposal, I wouldn't be able to commit to being able to support it or not support it. But we are committed to delivering on the government's objectives, affordability and security of supply. And so, uh, if there was a proposal and, uh, we were working with, with MBIE, and it would go through a normal process to be considered.

It's not a proposal. It's, uh, the Energy Wellbeing Evaluation Consortium has been an operational success. It, it, the only way- Yeah.... that we could have- I think we covered that.... uh, could do it is through our baseline funding. Yeah. If there's, if there are sufficient funds within their baseline funding- Yeah. ... we would actually lean in and support the communities to do that.

But that's the only vehicle we've got, unless there is a specific appropriation. Mm-hmm. And, Tha- thank you. Thank you. Look, I think we've got a supplementary over here on that. Grant? Yeah. I just, um, I'm curious how the, um, how it's being received, uh, in, in the sort of regional breakdown. Sort of been, uh, for particular- uh, the more highly deprived areas like, um, MBIE for Northland- Yeah.

... how it's being received, how you make, progress you make in areas like that? Really well received. So I work with Peter in Foundation North- Mm-hmm.... pretty closely. I've met with Dylan recently, the new CE there. Mm-hmm. A lot of the work that we do is through, um, philanthropic or third-party funders who have more of a community connection.

But, uh, the programs, either the advice or Warmer Kiwi Homes, uh-... has been very well received there. It's a, it's a challenging area because, um, of the remoteness. Mm-hmm. But I think all parts of New Zealand should be able to benefit from these programs. I'm also proud of our recent work in the Chatham Islands, and being able to bring warmer Kiwi homes insulation and efficient heating there.

And just to quickly lift up, and who, there are... you got enough providers to actually deliver the services in the north- Yeah.... for example? Yes. A- and we... it's not only foundation that we're working on in Northland. Uh, we work through the Ngapuhi tribes in Troon. So, Triarm's been very supportive of us. Thank So, we are actually getting to those hard-to-abate areas through those connections to the iwi communities.

Thank you. Thank you. Look, we'll go back to Megan Woods for a primary question. Thank you. Thank you. Um, EECA has a particular role to play in energy efficiency, um, as we go through a period of time where cost and security of supply- Mm-hmm.... are front of mind for both households and businesses. Um, I wonder, um, I know there's a, there's a few elements that I'd like to explore on this.

But the first is your flexibility pilots, um, your distributed flexibility pilots that you've been doing. In your annual report, um, you talk about the potential that a flexible electricity system could save New Zealand 10 billion by 2050. Uh, could you talk us through some of the work that you've been doing in these pilots, and where you think we're seeing the most promise?

This is like one of my favorite topics. (laughs) I'm gonna be... i- one of my favorite topics. (laughs) (laughs) I am gonna be very pithy, but if members want to... (laughs) (laughs)... uh, they can always come in and get me a pint afterwards, and I'll go on and on. (laughs) (laughs) (laughs) She is. So, Chair, I think you need to hold him back on this one as well. (laughs) Couldn't resist. Flexibility is just about shifting when demand is used. Uh, and the reason why it is so important from our perspective is that the asset, the, the poles and wires that get

built out, they're not being fully used all the time. They're kind of getting used maybe like up to their maximum 5% of the time. So, you end up with over-spec'd, under-utilized assets, and they're costly. So, any time that you can sort of smudge out demand and level it out a bit, it means that you get better utilization of your assets.

And it means that as a lines company, you're able to defer or plan out a much sort of longer tail of asset replacement, asset improvements. And that's where significant... that's where the significant bulk of the 10 billion figure comes from, is in this kind of better asset management. On top of that, you obviously get people saving money in their homes, people keeping money in their back pockets.

So, to answer your question, we've started off by partnering with six of the lines companies, given that's where we think a lot of the value st- sits, the large ones, um, Vector, Powerco, Counties, et cetera. And what we are looking at is scaling up the effort to be able to really understand the value, but also inform our activity as a product regulator. So, because we look at energy performance standards for appliances, we need to know how, um, how these different pieces of kit respond to demand signals. And I just want to note that this isn't just for

one end of town that have EVs and solar panels and all that kind of stuff. We've been looking at retrofitting really cheap $150 controls on existing hot water cylinders. Basically, the Ripple control system, but next level. So, it's accessible for everyone, that kind of, this kind of flexibility.

And the early results from the pilots have found that households are saving 7 or 8% off their bills. Oh, wow. 7 or 8%? 7 to 8%- Yeah.... off bills right now. And that, and- ？ That doesn't even count all, you know, all the system benefits- Yeah.... that will come up- Yeah. Mm-hmm.... with, uh, network asset defer.

Yeah. So, what could governments do to accelerate the uptake of some of the, the things that are coming out of your trials, like, for example, these $150, um, retrofits to hot water cylinders? Mm-hmm. Interesting. We, we are big fans of home energy management systems.

But, um, I know the Electricity Authority is doing a lot of work to try and send the right kind of market signals and price signals. From where we sit, as a product regulator, what we think is absolutely critical to get done as soon as possible is the amendment to the Energy Efficiency and Conservation Act- Yeah.

... to- Mm-hmm.... essentially do two things. One, ensure that smartness and connectivity is included as part of the criteria for energy performance, because at the moment, it's not. And two, to make our regime fit for purpose, so that when the executive and cabinet make a decision, we can enact it. So, at the moment, th- when the minister and cabinet make a decision with, jointly with Australia, because we developed standards jointly with Australia, it can sometimes take years, seven years, for that to go through the drafting

process and become a regulation for us. So, I don't think that is fit for purpose. It is a huge gap. The government has signed off an amendment. So, the government recognizes that this is a problem that needs to be fixed. They've agreed to fix it. That just needs to now be introduced into the House and go through the legislative process.

So, we would... We're gonna wa- I'm gonna watch that very closely and hopefully it'll progress as quickly as possible. Do you have an indication what, um, what, um, priority rating the leg has been given? No. No, I can't turn. Okay. No. Okay. We should get it in by the end of next year.

Sure. Can I possess that from me here on that? So- so- so just to conf- so effectively, you just want to be... the, the, the leads to be restructured so that you can just make regulatory changes easily and quickly that are required? That, that the cabinet, that, that when the Cabinet makes that- When the Cabinet say yes to it, then bank. Bank. As opposed to have to go through a long process. Yeah. There's a lot of red tape.

Yeah. Okay. We'll go to Scott Willum- It was a good sub. ... and then we'll come back. Megan, yeah. Excuse me. Yeah. So, I want to come back to, um, to healthy homes.... briefly. The, um, EECA, EECA did manage- Yeah.... some MBIE funds for the COVID, COVID recovery response- Okay.... in both Otago and the Far North.

And that, they were pilots looking at what a deep retrofit program would look like. Hm. I know that EECA has had cuts in funding over the last two budgets, but, um, sh- should there be a budget uplift at any point in the future, is that deep retrofit program that can particularly reach out to the hard-to-reach something that EECA is able to, to put back on the table? Is... Would some- Is that something that you would be interested in, in, in bringing back and having run those pilots and seeing what came out of them?

If the governments chose to actually stand it up, we could stand it up and deliver- Sure.... but political decision. Yeah. We could- We could-... execute it. We could execute what we are asked for from the government quickly. So it is a, it'll be a political decision as to whether they want to priori- prioritize that program or not.

But we have all of the machinery and muscle to do that. Okay. So there's a sub- Yeah.... sub to that. So given the, the number of Warmer Kiwi Home installations were 24,866, I think- Mm-hmm.... um, below the target. Is that, um, due to lower demand for heating- Yeah.

... installations or, and, and I guess what, what plans are there for using those unused funds to, to, to get to places? Yeah. So we were slightly below targets. That target is a blended target between efficient heating and installations. So the efficient heating portion of it was the bit that was really under target and kind of kept us down.

So what we've made changes this year is to reproportion more towards installation, less on efficient heating, um, 'cause we think a lot of heat pumps are getting high penetration rate through the market. It's a multi-year appropriation, so anything that might be underspent will be able to get carried forward an- and used.

So- (clears throat)... plans are in place to this year, this year to ensure we h- we hit the target. Um, thank you. Look, we'll come back to Megan for a question. Okay. Thank you. Um, so I want to continue on that theme about the role that ke- that EECA can play in helping make our energy system more affordable and more secure. There's obviously your flexibility pilots. Glad you got to talk about that, Marcos.

There's so much more to talk about than we- (laughs)... I may come back to it if there's time. Um, the other piece of work that I'm really interested in is what you can do with industry. So, of course, the RETA, the Regional Energy Transition Accelerators, if I recall it- Mm-hmm.... um, have completed their work.

There's an awful lot of data, um, that was produced out of the RETAs. What's happening with that information now and how are we picking it up so that we can help, um, keep jobs in, in the regions of New Zealand? Yeah. So Wellington region is getting finalized.

Then there will be an all of New Zealand RETA summary report that is gonna come out very shortly. And, uh, that will be the natural conclusion of that program. But we are use... I mean, the insights are freely available for anybody to use, but we are using it in terms of our targeting. So to try and take the next, the so what of doing the reports. So for example, we put out to market, um, a biomass hub RFP. So that is looking at unlocking supply and demand and addressing the market coordination problem. We are targeting areas that we know through RETA

have high biomass potential- Yep.... for both supply and demand. Uh, we've also, last winter, this, this previous winter, we were on the ground, boots on the ground, running, um, I don't like to use the word workshops 'cause people have different opinion of ideas of what a workshop is. We were running action meetings with businesses in each of the regions to help them reduce their energy, uh, right now. And we were using insights from RETA about where those businesses were, but also what their options are.

So we started with energy efficiency first, not surprisingly. We did supporting audits, supporting walkthrough, um, assessments. We found a lot of businesses were able to save 10 to 30% off the bat. That helped them right now. Mm-hmm. And then we talked to them about planning for what their options might be should they want to do something else. And using the insights from RETA helps inform, um, those, those discussions.

So I think I mean, hugely value- Sorry, Eleanor. The v- the value of RETA actually came to the fore when, um, the work we did around the East Coast of the North Island identified who were the gas users- Mm-hmm.... and the opportunities of them moving and shifting when gas was at a short supply. So that baseline work that was done several years ago was invaluable in terms of the opportunities of how we could actually shift them to other fuel sources.

Thank you. And that is a perfect segue way that, um, with it, there's such a rich body of knowledge that sits within the, the RETA work. Um, has government requested, um, any insight from EECA around what a gas prioritization plan might look like so that we were, um, running a New Zealand best interest test over how we do it and looking at what we could do to transition some of the, the cheapest and the easiest, um, transitions that would be...

Have you been asked for advice by ministers for that?You know, so I- w- I have not been working on a gas prioritization plan. Mm-hmm. Um, I think that's a question for MBIE. Yeah. But we have provided our reader data and all of those insights to MBIE to help inform the policy process.

Your work that you've done around energy efficiency, um, as you've identified, can save, um, huge amounts for- for businesses. Um, often that's seen in terms of their- um, their electricity use and- and electricity efficiency. Um, is there scope and capability f- within ECA to run a parallel gas efficiency program that would mean that businesses, um, can still get what they need but reduce their usage and their costs?

Absolutely. 100%. So energy is fuel agnostic, if that makes any sense. So we, whether- if it's somebody burning coal or somebody using gas, we are here to help them make the most of their energies. So to get us through this transition- Mm-hmm. ... um, that it would be possible for us to scale up what we're doing with some of our gas industrial users, um, so that they can save money. Yeah, and it complements this small fund we have around emerging technologies, whether it's body biomass or biogas.

Mm-hmm. What is the- the- the funding that you have in that area, Ella? So it's- Very small.... it's the te- it's the Technology and Fuel Enabler program. Oh yeah. So that's supporting the biomass work. And how much is it? Um- Sorry to-... it is- You can come back to us. No, I- I- I can look it up.... I can tell you. It's, uh, budgeted for approximately 20 million each.

Okay, so it's- A supplementary on that Yeah.... and then another supplement as well. So, um, obviously one of the bigger gas users particularly in- in the North Island is Fonterra. Mm-hmm. At the moment. Have you been ... I know- I know i- as a supplier, they've actually been working through and- and actually the sale of the brands process is gonna allow them to speed up some of that, um, um, that work.

Have you been working with them alongside to help structure what they've got? Absolutely. Yeah. And- We- we work through that notwithstanding that that fund is actually now closed. But a lot of the work of encouraging Fonterra to accelerate, um, moving away from coal in the South Island was initiated through our relationship with Fonterra.

Y- and- and so- and so that's been and that- and now even though- and now they have to continue to do that themselves regardless of government support in this space. They've got that funding. So they- They've got the- they've got the technology and they now feel more comfortable about applying that technology. Right. Mm-hmm. And we've done that, the same with other, um, other mil- companies that they've got operating in the woody biomass space.

The- the oth- the other area I think relevant to farmers and Fonterra or agriculture in general is solar. Yeah. Solar matters and so we- we are looking ... we have a program called Solar on Farms. It is looking at accelerating generation, uh, with growers and farmers and that, uh, that has been really well received by the market as well.

Mm-hmm. Just a quick supple- so is that- is that for them to use internally on their own properties? Yeah. To offset their use- Correct, yeah.... own- own usage as opposed to- It's- ... as opposed to putting in solar farms to export? It's not a- and it's not a community-based one. Okay. It's a specific- And- and it's a demonstration. Yeah. Because, uh, there is a very, um, s- you know, there's a cherry orchardist who is very passionate about solar (laughs) Uh, might what- what might work for a cherry orchard may not work for

a piggery, may not work for a milking shed. That's Mm-hmm. So a lot of the farmers we've talked to said actually they want to understand the cost and benefits of the system for their type of business. Business, yeah.
